texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: arXiv:2312.10183v3  [physics.flu-dyn]  16 Dec 2025
texts.text: arXiv:2312.10183v3  [physics.flu-dyn]  16 Dec 2025

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We demonstrate the use of the Lorentz reciprocal theorem in obtaining corrections to the steady flow rate due to flow oscillations in rigid channels. Starting from the unsteady Stokes equations, we derive the suitable reciprocity relation, assuming all quantities can be expressed as time-harmonic phasors. The auxiliary problem is the steady Hagen-Poiseuille flow solution, from which the reciprocal theorem allows us to calculate the first-order correction in the Womersley number to the steady flow rate in a straight rigid channel. We also consider nonuniform channels, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction, in which case the flow rate correction provides the leading-order effect of the interplay between the oscillations of the fluid flow and the given shape of the channel.
texts.text: We demonstrate the use of the Lorentz reciprocal theorem in obtaining corrections to the steady flow rate due to flow oscillations in rigid channels. Starting from the unsteady Stokes equations, we derive the suitable reciprocity relation, assuming all quantities can be expressed as time-harmonic phasors. The auxiliary problem is the steady Hagen-Poiseuille flow solution, from which the reciprocal theorem allows us to calculate the first-order correction in the Womersley number to the steady flow rate in a straight rigid channel. We also consider nonuniform channels, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction, in which case the flow rate correction provides the leading-order effect of the interplay between the oscillations of the fluid flow and the given shape of the channel.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Oscillatory channel flow, Reciprocal theorem, Flow rate
texts.text: Keywords: Oscillatory channel flow, Reciprocal theorem, Flow rate

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Low-Reynolds-number oscillatory flows are widely encountered in microfluidics applications such as the mixing of fluids [1], the generation of emulsions, and particle or cell separation [2]. Traditionally, the channels in these applications [3, 4, 2, 5] are considered rigid, and it is of interest to determine the hydraulic impedance [6] (i.e., the complex hydrodynamic resistance [7]) due to the oscillatory flow.
texts.text: Low-Reynolds-number oscillatory flows are widely encountered in microfluidics applications such as the mixing of fluids [1], the generation of emulsions, and particle or cell separation [2]. Traditionally, the channels in these applications [3, 4, 2, 5] are considered rigid, and it is of interest to determine the hydraulic impedance [6] (i.e., the complex hydrodynamic resistance [7]) due to the oscillatory flow.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accurate modeling and design of such microsystems requires a quantitative understanding of the relationship between key integrated quantities, such as flow rate and pressure drop, in oscillatory flow. One approach to obtaining such relations is to solve the 'complete' problem, meaning to solve the governing unsteady hydrodynamic equations. Another useful approach for finding integrated quantities (such as force, torque, or flow rate) is to exploit the Lorentz reciprocal theorem [8], which relates two different flow problems to each other (see, e.g., [9, 10] and the references therein). For example, in this way, a known solution of a 'simpler' problem (termed the auxiliary problem) can be used to obtain the flow rate of a 'harder' problem without the need for calculating its velocity and pressure fields.
texts.text: Accurate modeling and design of such microsystems requires a quantitative understanding of the relationship between key integrated quantities, such as flow rate and pressure drop, in oscillatory flow. One approach to obtaining such relations is to solve the 'complete' problem, meaning to solve the governing unsteady hydrodynamic equations. Another useful approach for finding integrated quantities (such as force, torque, or flow rate) is to exploit the Lorentz reciprocal theorem [8], which relates two different flow problems to each other (see, e.g., [9, 10] and the references therein). For example, in this way, a known solution of a 'simpler' problem (termed the auxiliary problem) can be used to obtain the flow rate of a 'harder' problem without the need for calculating its velocity and pressure fields.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This approach is particularly well-suited for low-Reynoldsnumber fluid mechanics applications such as those described above. For example, the reciprocal theorem has been used to predict the forces on and velocities of particles [11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17] and disks [11, 18] in oscillating flow fields. Although many applications of the reciprocal theorem are to threedimensional flows, two-dimensional (2D) external oscillatory flows caused by the motion of immersed surfaces can also be handled [19, 20]. In the present context, 2D flows are an idealization of 3D flows that are unconfined (or of large extent) in the spanwise direction perpendicular to the flow. Then, the lubrication approximation for slender flow geometries [21] suggests that the velocity varies transversely and the pressure varies axially, and the reciprocal theorem has been used to obtain closedform analytical expressions for flow rate-pressure drop relations of steady non-Newtonian flows through rigid [22, 23] and deformable [24, 25, 26] channels.
texts.text: This approach is particularly well-suited for low-Reynoldsnumber fluid mechanics applications such as those described above. For example, the reciprocal theorem has been used to predict the forces on and velocities of particles [11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17] and disks [11, 18] in oscillating flow fields. Although many applications of the reciprocal theorem are to threedimensional flows, two-dimensional (2D) external oscillatory flows caused by the motion of immersed surfaces can also be handled [19, 20]. In the present context, 2D flows are an idealization of 3D flows that are unconfined (or of large extent) in the spanwise direction perpendicular to the flow. Then, the lubrication approximation for slender flow geometries [21] suggests that the velocity varies transversely and the pressure varies axially, and the reciprocal theorem has been used to obtain closedform analytical expressions for flow rate-pressure drop relations of steady non-Newtonian flows through rigid [22, 23] and deformable [24, 25, 26] channels.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However, despite all the progress made, to the best of our knowledge, no application of the reciprocal theorem to oscillatory channel flows has been presented to date. This observation motivates our study, which aims to show the reciprocal theorem's versatility and simplicity for studying the coupling between flow oscillations and channel shape.
texts.text: However, despite all the progress made, to the best of our knowledge, no application of the reciprocal theorem to oscillatory channel flows has been presented to date. This observation motivates our study, which aims to show the reciprocal theorem's versatility and simplicity for studying the coupling between flow oscillations and channel shape.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To this end, in Sec. 2, we formulate the problem of lowReynolds-number flow in two-dimensional channels, and construct a suitable reciprocal theorem relating an oscillatory flow to a steady flow. Next, in Sec. 3, we introduce the lubrication approximation and show how the reciprocal theorem can be used to obtain the leading-order correction (in the Womersley number) to the flow rate due to flow oscillations. The calculation is then implemented for a straight 2D rigid channel in Sec. 4 and for a nonuniform 2D rigid channel, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction, in Sec. 5. Finally, Sec. 6 concludes our study.
texts.text: To this end, in Sec. 2, we formulate the problem of lowReynolds-number flow in two-dimensional channels, and construct a suitable reciprocal theorem relating an oscillatory flow to a steady flow. Next, in Sec. 3, we introduce the lubrication approximation and show how the reciprocal theorem can be used to obtain the leading-order correction (in the Womersley number) to the flow rate due to flow oscillations. The calculation is then implemented for a straight 2D rigid channel in Sec. 4 and for a nonuniform 2D rigid channel, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction, in Sec. 5. Finally, Sec. 6 concludes our study.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Fig. 1(b)). We assume that the axial length of the channels, ℓ , is much greater than the channels' height h , i.e., we consider slender geometries. The viscous fluid flow is driven by an imposed oscillatory pressure difference with amplitude ∆ p and frequency ω , resulting in a velocity field v and a pressure distribution p . Our goal is to determine the flow rate q , and specifically how it depends on ω (or, dimensionless groups involving ω ), without solving the oscillatory flow problem.
texts.text: (Fig. 1(b)). We assume that the axial length of the channels, ℓ , is much greater than the channels' height h , i.e., we consider slender geometries. The viscous fluid flow is driven by an imposed oscillatory pressure difference with amplitude ∆ p and frequency ω , resulting in a velocity field v and a pressure distribution p . Our goal is to determine the flow rate q , and specifically how it depends on ω (or, dimensionless groups involving ω ), without solving the oscillatory flow problem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where I is the identity tensor, E = 1 2 [ ∇ v + ( ∇ v ) ⊤ ] is the rate-of-strain tensor, µ is the fluid's dynamic viscosity, and ρ is the fluid's density. To retain the unsteady term but neglect the convective inertia, as in Eq. (1), we introduce the Reynolds number Re = ρ vch 0 /µ and the Womersley number Wo = h 0 p ρω/µ [28, 27] and require that Re ≪ Wo 2 (or, equivalently, vc /ω ≪ h 0, i.e., the flow oscillation amplitude is much smaller than the channel height) [11]. Here, vc is the characteristic axial velocity scale, and h 0 is either the rigid channel's height or the inlet / outlet height of the nonuniform channel.

(1), we introduce the Reynolds number Re = ρ vch 0 /µ and the Womersley number Wo = h 0 p ρω/µ [28, 27] and require that Re ≪ Wo 2 (or, equivalently, vc /ω ≪ h 0, i.e., the flow oscillation amplitude is much smaller than the channel height) [11]. Here, vc is the characteristic axial velocity scale, and h 0 is either the rigid channel's height or the inlet / outlet height of the nonuniform channel.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the two planar (2D) geometries considered: (a) a straight rigid channel and (b) a nonuniform rigid channel, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction. The straight channel has a height h 0, which is also the inlet / outlet height of the nonuniform channel. The integration domain V for the reciprocal theorem is the shaded interior of the channels. Its boundary is marked with bright dotted curves; S 0 and S ℓ denote the inlet and outlet surfaces, respectively. The channels' axial length is ℓ ≫ h . An oscillatory viscous fluid flow is driven by an imposed pressure difference, ℜ [ ∆ p e i ω t ], with the outlet ( z = ℓ ) pressure setting the gage. The fluid velocity satisfies the no-slip condition along the stationary top and bottom walls of the channels (i.e., v = 0 there). We are interested in determining the resulting flow rate q .
texts.text: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the two planar (2D) geometries considered: (a) a straight rigid channel and (b) a nonuniform rigid channel, specifically with variable height in the flow-wise direction. The straight channel has a height h 0, which is also the inlet / outlet height of the nonuniform channel. The integration domain V for the reciprocal theorem is the shaded interior of the channels. Its boundary is marked with bright dotted curves; S 0 and S ℓ denote the inlet and outlet surfaces, respectively. The channels' axial length is ℓ ≫ h . An oscillatory viscous fluid flow is driven by an imposed pressure difference, ℜ [ ∆ p e i ω t ], with the outlet ( z = ℓ ) pressure setting the gage. The fluid velocity satisfies the no-slip condition along the stationary top and bottom walls of the channels (i.e., v = 0 there). We are interested in determining the resulting flow rate q .

texts.orig: Equation (11) clearly shows that the dimensionless amplitude of the flow rate (or pressure drop) depends on Vz , a , and thus, generally, requires solving the complete oscillatory flow problem. However, in the weakly oscillatory limit, corresponding to Wo 2 ≪ 1, we can apply the reciprocal theorem (11) to obtain the leading-order oscillatory correction to the flow rate using only the steady solution of the problem, as also observed in [15, 17]. texts.text: Equation (11) clearly shows that the dimensionless amplitude of the flow rate (or pressure drop) depends on Vz , a , and thus, generally, requires solving the complete oscillatory flow problem. However, in the weakly oscillatory limit, corresponding to Wo 2 ≪ 1, we can apply the reciprocal theorem (11) to obtain the leading-order oscillatory correction to the flow rate using only the steady solution of the problem, as also observed in [15, 17]. To this end, we expand the amplitude of the velocity into a perturbation series in the dimensionless parameter Wo 2 ≪ 1,

(1) and (3)). The remaining calculations are thus all accurate to O(Wo 2 ).

(1) and (3)). The remaining calculations are thus all accurate to O(Wo 2 ).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this communication, we revisited a basic problem in fluid mechanics using the Lorentz reciprocal theorem. Specifically, we derived a closed-form integral relation for calculating the flow rate in rigid 2D channels, with constant or axially varying heights, conveying oscillatory flows of incompressible Newtonian fluids. Our main result is Eq. (11), which relates the flow rates and pressure drops of steady and oscillatory flows (in 2D channels) to each other. For weakly oscillatory flow, we showed how the steady (Hagen-Poiseuille) flow solution can be used as the auxiliary problem in the reciprocal theorem to obtain the oscillatory flow rate up to O(Wo 2 ). The flow rates obtained via the reciprocal theorem were verified by comparing to the lowWomersley-number asymptotic expressions stemming from the known analytical solutions (valid at any Womersley number). To the best of our knowledge, this admittedly simple but elegant calculation has not been done in the literature, highlighting the novelty of our approach.
texts.text: In this communication, we revisited a basic problem in fluid mechanics using the Lorentz reciprocal theorem. Specifically, we derived a closed-form integral relation for calculating the flow rate in rigid 2D channels, with constant or axially varying heights, conveying oscillatory flows of incompressible Newtonian fluids. Our main result is Eq. (11), which relates the flow rates and pressure drops of steady and oscillatory flows (in 2D channels) to each other. For weakly oscillatory flow, we showed how the steady (Hagen-Poiseuille) flow solution can be used as the auxiliary problem in the reciprocal theorem to obtain the oscillatory flow rate up to O(Wo 2 ). The flow rates obtained via the reciprocal theorem were verified by comparing to the lowWomersley-number asymptotic expressions stemming from the known analytical solutions (valid at any Womersley number). To the best of our knowledge, this admittedly simple but elegant calculation has not been done in the literature, highlighting the novelty of our approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Importantly, this approach allows one to bypass solving the momentum equation in obtaining the leading-order effect of flow oscillations. Thus, it would be straightforward to extend the present calculations for 2D channels to narrow axisymmetric tubes and three-dimensional (3D) channels of rectangular cross-section. Indeed, since the oscillatory pressure-driven flow field for the latter geometry is also known (see, e.g., [6] and the references therein), one can find the leading-order effect of the flow oscillations and the cross-sectional aspect ratio of the 3D channel on the flow rate.
texts.text: Importantly, this approach allows one to bypass solving the momentum equation in obtaining the leading-order effect of flow oscillations. Thus, it would be straightforward to extend the present calculations for 2D channels to narrow axisymmetric tubes and three-dimensional (3D) channels of rectangular cross-section. Indeed, since the oscillatory pressure-driven flow field for the latter geometry is also known (see, e.g., [6] and the references therein), one can find the leading-order effect of the flow oscillations and the cross-sectional aspect ratio of the 3D channel on the flow rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As an extension of this work, it would be of interest to adapt the reciprocal theorem to oscillatory flows in compliant conduits [32, 33, 34]. Furthermore, as a future research direction, one could use the reciprocal theorem to explore how the rheological response of complex fluids, such as shear thinning and viscoelasticity, affects the oscillatory flow rate in both rigid and deformable channels.
texts.text: As an extension of this work, it would be of interest to adapt the reciprocal theorem to oscillatory flows in compliant conduits [32, 33, 34]. Furthermore, as a future research direction, one could use the reciprocal theorem to explore how the rheological response of complex fluids, such as shear thinning and viscoelasticity, affects the oscillatory flow rate in both rigid and deformable channels.
